body:
## Summary

`docs/CONCEPTS-COMPARISION.md` is useful as a positioning document, but the current comparison matrix mixes several different levels of meaning:

- trusted kernel features;
- library-level features;
- object-logic encodings;
- host-language implementation details;
- runtime configuration;
- external trusted procedures;
- theoretical capabilities;
- prototype / partial features.

As a result, several `Yes` / `No` / `Part` entries are ambiguous or outdated, especially for RML itself.

This issue proposes revising the document so that it remains useful as a high-level comparison while being more technically precise and harder to misread.

## Motivation

The document compares systems with very different roles:

- RML — host-implemented configurable many-valued/probabilistic meta-logic;
- Twelf / LF — logical framework and LF implementation;
- Isabelle — generic prover with object logics such as HOL;
- Rocq/Coq and Lean — dependent type theory based proof assistants;
- Foundation — Lean 4 library for mathematical logic;
- AFP — archive of Isabelle developments;
- Abella — prover for two-level logic and lambda-tree syntax;
- lambda Prolog — higher-order hereditary Harrop logic programming language;
- Pecan — domain-specific automated prover for automatic sequences / Büchi automata.

A single `Yes` or `No` often means different things for different systems.

For example:

- Lean/Rocq may support a concept as a library-level construction, while the kernel itself remains fixed.
- Isabelle may support a concept through an object logic, while Isabelle/Pure remains a separate meta-logic.
- RML may support a concept natively in the evaluator, but the underlying semantics may still be host-implemented.
- Foundation and AFP should usually be marked as library/archive content rather than as independent prover implementations.
- Pecan has strong support for its decidable domain, but not for general-purpose proof assistant concepts.

The matrix should make these distinctions explicit.

## Main problem

The current legend is too coarse:

```md
Yes
Part
Host
Theory
N/A
No
```

These categories are not enough to distinguish:

- kernel-level support;
- library-level support;
- support by encoding;
- runtime-configurable support;
- host-implemented support;
- external trusted support;
- prototype support;
- theoretical but not implemented support.

This creates misleading entries such as:

- `Redefinable logical operators: Yes` for Lean/Rocq/Isabelle, even though their kernel/meta-logic semantics are not runtime-redefinable in the RML sense.
- `Full normalization: No` for RML, although RML currently exposes `whnf`, `nf`, and `normal-form` for the typed lambda fragment.
- `Inductive families: Part: encodable only` for RML, although the current typed kernel documentation describes implemented `(inductive ...)` declarations and generated recursors.
- `Independent proof replay: No` for RML, although the current soundness documentation describes a proof replay checker.
- `No ATP bridge` in the RML positioning table, although the current kernel/tactic documentation describes an ATP bridge with trusted ATP nodes.

## Proposed changes

### 1. Expand the legend

Replace or extend the current legend with more precise markers.

Suggested markers:

| Mark | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `Kernel` | Built into the trusted kernel or meta-kernel of the system. |
| `Library` | Available through a checked library or standard development, not kernel-native. |
| `Encoding` | Can be represented as an object logic or encoded structure. |
| `Runtime` | Selected by runtime configuration rather than fixed kernel semantics. |
| `Host` | Implemented by the host implementation rather than defined in the object language. |
| `External` | Delegated to an external trusted tool or procedure. |
| `Prototype` | Implemented partially or experimentally; not mature. |
| `Theory` | Theoretical framework, not a standalone implementation. |
| `N/A` | Not applicable to the system's role. |
| `No` | No evidence of support or outside the system's design goal. |

The existing `Yes` / `Part` labels may remain, but they should be qualified where necessary:

```md
Yes (Kernel)
Yes (Library)
Part (Prototype)
Part (Encoding)
Yes (Runtime + Host)
```

### 2. Separate systems, libraries, and archives

The document currently lists Foundation and AFP in the same table as full systems.

This is understandable for broad positioning, but the distinction should be explicit.

Suggested revision:

```md
## Systems and artifacts

### Provers / frameworks / languages
- RML
- Twelf
- Edinburgh LF
- HELF
- Isabelle
- Rocq/Coq
- Lean
- Abella
- lambda Prolog
- Pecan

### Libraries / archives
- Foundation
- AFP
```

For Foundation and AFP entries, prefer `Host`, `Library`, or `Archive`-style wording rather than direct `Yes` when the capability comes from Lean/Isabelle.

### 3. Clarify kernel vs object logic vs library support

Some entries should be rewritten to avoid implying that object-level encodings are the same as kernel-level features.

Examples:

#### Classical logic

Current style:

```md
Coq/Rocq: Yes
Lean: Yes
```

Suggested wording:

```md
Coq/Rocq: Library/Axiom — classical reasoning available, kernel remains CIC.
Lean: Library/Axiom — classical reasoning available, kernel remains DTT.
Isabelle: Yes — Isabelle/HOL is classical HOL; Isabelle/Pure remains the meta-logic.
```

#### Redefinable logical operators

Current style:

```md
Isabelle: Yes
Coq/Rocq: Yes
Lean: Yes
```

Suggested wording:

```md
RML: Runtime — operator table and aggregators are runtime-configurable.
Isabelle: Encoding/Library — object logics and notation can be defined; Pure meta-logic is fixed.
Coq/Rocq: Library/Notation — new connectives and notation can be defined; CIC kernel is fixed.
Lean: Library/Notation — new connectives, notation, typeclass instances, and macros can be defined; kernel rules are fixed.
Twelf/LF: Encoding — object judgments/connectives are encoded as LF constants/families; LF core is fixed.
```

This would make RML's runtime reconfiguration model clearer without overstating other systems.

### 4. Update RML entries that are stale

Several RML rows appear outdated relative to the current RML documentation.

Suggested updates:

#### Full normalization

Current:

```md
RML: No: evaluator is not a normalizer
```

Suggested:

```md
RML: Part / Prototype — `whnf`, `nf`, and `normal-form` exist for the typed lambda fragment; the whole evaluator is not a general normalizer for all RML semantics.
```

#### Inductive families

Current:

```md
RML: Part: encodable only
```

Suggested:

```md
RML: Part / Prototype — `(inductive ...)` declarations and generated recursors exist; strict positivity and mature dependent pattern machinery remain limited.
```

#### Coinductive types/predicates

Current:

```md
RML: Part: encodable only
```

Suggested:

```md
RML: Part / Prototype — `(coinductive ...)` declarations and generated corecursors exist, with productivity checks; proof theory and tooling remain immature.
```

#### Totality / termination / coverage checking

Current:

```md
RML: No
```

Suggested:

```md
RML: Part / Prototype — totality, termination, coverage, and mode-checking are present in the current metatheorem-checking/self layer; clarify which checks are host-enforced and which are encoded as data.
```

If some of these are only encoded in the self-bootstrap layer and not enforced by the host checker, say so explicitly.

#### Tactic-level proof construction

Current:

```md
RML: No
```

Suggested:

```md
RML: Part / Prototype — tactic links exist (`reflexivity`, `symmetry`, `transitivity`, `rewrite`, `simplify`, `smt`, `atp`, `exact`, `induction`, etc.), but the tactic layer is not mature like Lean/Rocq/Isabelle.
```

#### Proof-producing evaluator

Current:

```md
RML: No
```

Suggested:

```md
RML: Part / Prototype — selected queries can produce proof links; semantic computation still depends on trusted evaluator primitives.
```

#### Independent proof replay

Current:

```md
RML: No
```

Suggested:

```md
RML: Part / Prototype — independent proof replay exists for current proof-link rule families, but it is not a fully mechanised metatheory.
```

#### External certification bridge

Current:

```md
RML: No
```

Suggested:

```md
RML: Part / Trusted external — SMT and ATP bridges exist, but successful decisions are recorded as trusted external nodes rather than independently replayed proof certificates.
```

#### ATP bridge in positioning table

Current gap:

```md
no ATP bridge
```

Suggested:

```md
ATP bridge exists, but ATP results are trusted external nodes rather than independently certified proofs.
```

### 5. Clarify RML's numeric and many-valued semantics

Rows such as:

```md
Numeric truth values in the core: Yes
Configurable semantic range: Yes
Configurable valence: Yes
Fuzzy logic: Yes
Probabilistic truth values: Yes
Probabilistic operators: Yes
```

are broadly correct at the evaluator/configuration level, but they should make clear that the numeric semantics are currently host-implemented.

Suggested wording:

```md
Numeric truth values in the core:
RML: Yes (Runtime + Host) — numeric truth values are native to the evaluator; numeric domains and arithmetic are currently host-implemented.

Configurable semantic range:
RML: Yes (Runtime + Host) — range is configurable, but interval endpoints, midpoint, order, clamping, and arithmetic are host-defined.

Configurable valence:
RML: Yes (Runtime + Host) — valence and quantization are runtime-configurable; quantization arithmetic is host-implemented.

Fuzzy logic:
RML: Yes (Runtime + Host) — continuous truth values and aggregators are supported, but their numeric basis is host-defined.

Probabilistic operators:
RML: Yes (Runtime + Host) — product and probabilistic-sum aggregators exist; their arithmetic semantics are host-defined.
```

This keeps the comparison honest while still acknowledging RML's distinctive runtime flexibility.

### 6. Clarify equality-related entries

The row:

```md
Structural equality over expressions: Yes
```

is too coarse because RML has several equality-like layers:

- structural equality;
- explicit assigned equality;
- numeric equality;
- definitional equality / convertibility;
- object-level equality inside encoded systems.

Suggested addition:

```md
RML has multiple equality layers: structural equality, assigned equality, numeric equality, and beta/definitional convertibility. The comparison matrix should specify which one is meant in each row.
```

Possible new row:

```md
Equality layers distinguished
```

Suggested entries:

```md
RML: Part — several equality mechanisms exist, but the documentation should make their proof-theoretic roles explicit.
Lean/Rocq: Yes — definitional equality and propositional equality are clearly separated.
Isabelle: Yes — meta-equality / object equality distinctions are logic-dependent.
Twelf/LF: Yes — definitional equality / type equality are part of LF checking.
```

### 7. Avoid overclaiming about self-reference

Current:

```md
Self-reference accepted by default: Yes: midpoint/paradox-tolerant evaluation
```

Suggested:

```md
RML: Yes (Runtime semantics) — self-referential/circular forms can be accepted and evaluated through many-valued/paradox-tolerant semantics. This should not be read as a classical consistency or mechanised soundness claim.
```

This prevents readers from confusing paradox-tolerant evaluation with a stronger metatheoretic guarantee.

### 8. Refine lambda Prolog and Twelf rows

#### lambda Prolog

Rows involving “higher-order logic” should avoid implying HOL-style theorem proving.

Suggested wording:

```md
lambda Prolog: higher-order hereditary Harrop logic programming with lambda terms as data and higher-order abstract syntax; not HOL in the Isabelle/HOL sense.
```

#### Twelf

Twelf has proof search, type checking, reconstruction, modes, worlds, termination, coverage, and totality checking, but not tactics in the Lean/Rocq/Isabelle sense.

Suggested correction:

```md
Tactic-level proof construction:
Twelf: No / N/A — proof search and metatheorem checking exist, but not tactic-level interactive proof construction in the Lean/Rocq/Isabelle style.
```

### 9. Add a status note for RML

A short note near the RML row would help:

```md
RML status note:
RML currently combines host-implemented evaluator/kernel machinery with `.lino`-level encodings and runtime configuration. A `Yes` for RML often means “available in the current evaluator/runtime”, not necessarily “defined inside `.lino` with a mechanised metatheory”.
```

This would prevent overreading the matrix.

### 10. Fix naming / spelling issues

The filename and linked document name use:

```text
CONCEPTS-COMPARISION.md
FEATURE-COMPARISION.md
```

The standard spelling is:

```text
COMPARISON
```

Possible options:

1. Rename the files to:
   - `CONCEPTS-COMPARISON.md`
   - `FEATURE-COMPARISON.md`

2. Keep old filenames for compatibility and add redirects/links if necessary.

This is a minor documentation cleanup, but it is worth doing while updating the file.

## Suggested acceptance criteria

This issue can be considered complete when:

- [ ] The legend distinguishes kernel/library/encoding/runtime/host/external/prototype/theory support.
- [ ] Foundation and AFP are clearly marked as library/archive artifacts rather than standalone prover kernels.
- [ ] RML rows are updated to reflect current documentation for proof replay, ATP bridge, tactics, normalization, inductive/coinductive declarations, and metatheorem checks.
- [ ] RML numeric, fuzzy, probabilistic, and many-valued entries explicitly say whether they are runtime/host-defined rather than fully object-language-defined.
- [ ] Lean/Rocq/Isabelle entries distinguish kernel semantics from library-level definitions and object-logic encodings.
- [ ] “Redefinable logical operators” is rewritten to avoid implying that Lean/Rocq/Isabelle have RML-style runtime-redefinable kernel connectives.
- [ ] Equality-related rows distinguish structural equality, definitional equality, numeric equality, and object-level equality where relevant.
- [ ] Self-reference/paradox-tolerance is described as evaluator/runtime behavior, not as a classical consistency or fully mechanised soundness claim.
- [ ] The RML positioning table no longer says “no ATP bridge” or “no independent proof replay” if those features are present in the current implementation.
- [ ] The document includes a short note explaining that the matrix is positioning-oriented, not a formal metatheoretic equivalence claim.
- [ ] Optional: rename `COMPARISION` files to `COMPARISON` or add compatibility links.

## Non-goals

This issue is not asking to change the RML implementation.

It is only about making the comparison document more precise, current, and harder to misinterpret.

In particular, the goal is not to make RML look weaker or stronger than other systems, but to make each entry say *where* and *in what sense* a concept is supported.

comments.body:
We need to update our comparison with all other similar systems, by supported concepts, by supported features and so on.

Make sure comparison is verified with tests.

We need to collect data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), list of each and all requirements from the issue, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).

Please plan and execute everything in a single pull request, you have unlimited time and context, as context auto-compacts and you can continue indefinitely, until it is each and every requirement fully addressed, and everything is totally done.

title: Update `docs/CONCEPTS-COMPARISION.md` to make the concept matrix technically precise
